title: 1. Docker Introduction
explanation: Docker package tools isolate applications into container sandboxes containing system libraries, configs, and bin runtimes. Containers run natively on the host shared kernel.
instruction: Run docker --version to check if the Docker command line client is ready on your host system.

title: 2. Docker Architecture
explanation: Docker uses a client-server architecture. The Docker client talks to the Docker daemon (dockerd), which handles building, running, and distributing containers.
instruction: Query the Docker system-wide details to see running configurations using info.

title: 3. Docker Installation Check
explanation: A simple way to verify your installation and permissions is running a test container. The hello-world container downloads and prints a diagnosis check.
instruction: Launch a verification check container using run hello-world.
hint: Run exact command: docker run hello-world

title: 4. Images (pull & images)
explanation: Images are read-only templates used to create containers. The docker images command lists all cached images locally. docker pull downloads an image from Registry.
instruction: Pull a small Alpine Linux image and view your local images using pull and images commands.
expected: alpine list entry shows up under local images.
hint: Run exact command sequence: docker pull alpine && docker images

title: 5. Containers (run & ps)
explanation: Containers are runnable instances of images. Use docker run to launch containers, and docker ps to inspect running ones. The -d flag runs it detached in the background.
instruction: Launch a detached Nginx web server container named my_web running in background.
expected: Long container hex ID is printed.
hint: Run exact command: docker run -d --name my_web nginx

title: 6. Docker CLI (ps -a)
explanation: docker ps shows active containers. By default, stopped or exited containers are hidden. The -a flag lists all containers in the system.
instruction: List all containers in the sandbox environment including stopped/exited containers.
expected: List showing active Nginx and stopped hello-world containers.

title: 7. Dockerfile
explanation: A Dockerfile is a text document containing instructions to assemble an image. The FROM instruction sets the base image, and RUN runs commands.
instruction: Create a file named Dockerfile in your directory with FROM alpine and RUN echo 'Hello' instructions.

title: 8. Build & Layers
explanation: The docker build command compiles a Dockerfile into an image. Each instruction inside the Dockerfile creates a read-only Layer.
instruction: Build an image tagged custom_app:v1 using the Dockerfile in the current directory.
hint: Run exact command: docker build -t custom_app:v1 .

title: 9. Build Cache
explanation: During build, Docker reuses cached layers from previous builds if instructions haven't changed. This makes compilation extremely fast.
instruction: Re-run the build command for custom_app:v1 to see caching layers speed (Using cache).
hint: Run exact command: docker build -t custom_app:v1 .

title: 10. Volumes (persistence)
explanation: Containers are ephemeral. To persist and share data, Docker uses Volumes. Use docker volume create to define managed volumes.
instruction: Create a Docker volume named db_data to store db logs.
hint: Run exact command: docker volume create db_data

title: 11. Networks (isolation)
explanation: Docker Networks isolate container communications. The default is bridge, but user-defined networks enable secure DNS resolutions.
instruction: Create a custom bridge network named backend_net.
expected: backend_net is listed under docker network ls.
hint: Run exact command: docker network create backend_net

title: 12. Environment Variables (-e)
explanation: Docker allows configuring services using environment variables. The -e flag passes environment variables to containers.
instruction: Run a detached Nginx container named env_web with an environment variable PORT=8080.
expected: env_web container launched with PORT variable.
hint: Run exact command: docker run -d --name env_web -e PORT=8080 nginx

title: 13. Logs (debugging)
explanation: The docker logs command fetches stdout/stderr output from a background container.
instruction: Query logs from the my_web container to inspect startup stdout.
hint: Run exact command: docker logs my_web

title: 14. Exec (interactive shell)
explanation: The docker exec command runs a new command inside a running container. Use -it to open an interactive bash session.
instruction: Run a diagnostic check command (e.g., date) inside my_web using exec.
expected: Current system date from container printed.
hint: Run exact command: docker exec my_web date

title: 15. Inspect (metadata metadata)
explanation: docker inspect returns low-level system configuration descriptors in JSON format for containers, images, volumes or networks.
instruction: Inspect network properties of bridge using inspect.
expected: JSON array output with configuration parameters.
hint: Run exact command: docker inspect bridge

title: 16. Multi-stage Builds
explanation: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM blocks in a Dockerfile. You build components in early stages and copy artifacts to the final stage, reducing size.
instruction: Add a COPY --from instruction to build structures in a Dockerfile.
hint:
Create a file named Dockerfile.multistage containing: FROM alpine AS builder
RUN touch /app
FROM alpine
COPY --from=builder /app /app

title: 17. Stop & Cleanup (rm & prune)
explanation: Containers occupy resources. The docker stop command halts execution, and docker rm deletes containers. docker system prune cleans all unused caches.
instruction: Stop and remove the my_web container to free resources.
example: docker stop container_name  &&  docker rm container_name
expected: my_web container is stopped and deleted.
hint: Run exact commands: docker stop my_web && docker rm my_web

title: 18. Final Project (Docker Compose)
explanation: Docker Compose aggregates configurations into a single docker-compose.yml file to coordinate multi-container applications (e.g., web-app + database).
instruction: Write a docker-compose.yml file defining a web service running nginx and backend database service.
hint:
Create docker-compose.yml containing: version: '3'
services:
  web:
    image: nginx
  db:
    image: redis
